content:
Every Microsoft 365 E3 organisation already holds the licence to configure powerful Conditional Access (CA) policies today. Entra ID P1 is included in M365 E3, and it covers named locations, authentication strengths, device compliance grants, and app protection controls. That’s a real Zero Trust foundation, not just an MFA toggle. What E5 adds is automated, continuous risk response: Entra ID Protection feeds real-time sign-in and user risk signals directly into CA policy decisions. This guide walks through the full feature set, from policy anatomy to safe rollout, so you can build the right policies for the licences you hold now and make a clear-eyed decision about upgrading.
Conditional Access is the policy engine at the centre of Microsoft’s Zero Trust architecture. Photo: FlyD via Unsplash.
Key Takeaways
Conditional Access requires Entra ID P1, included in Microsoft 365 E3 and Business Premium. Risk-based policies (sign-in risk, user risk) require Entra ID P2, available in Microsoft 365 E5 or as an add-on. “Require approved client app” was retired in March 2026. Migrate existing policies to “Require app protection policy.” Microsoft recommends at least one week in report-only mode before enforcing any policy (Source: Microsoft Learn, retrieved 2026-05-22). CA App Control requires a separate Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps licence, not included in E3 or standalone P1/P2. What Is the Conditional Access Policy Model? Conditional Access uses a two-phase evaluation model for every sign-in attempt. Phase 1 collects session details: user identity, device state, location, client application, and the target resource. Phase 2 enforces the policies that match those collected signals. Assignments between categories use AND logic, so a user must satisfy all configured conditions for a policy to apply. Within grant controls, the default behaviour is “require all selected controls.” A single tenant can hold up to 240 policies across all states (enabled, disabled, and report-only). Source: Microsoft Learn, “What is Conditional Access”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Grant controls are evaluated in a fixed order when multiple grants are required: MFA, device marked as compliant, hybrid join, approved client app, app protection policy, password change, terms of use, then custom controls. Understanding that order matters when you stack controls. If any Block policy applies to a sign-in, it wins regardless of other grant policies in the chain. Keep your scope precise. A broad Block policy will catch more users than intended.
What Do You Need to Set Up Before Configuring Policies? Two things must be in place before you touch the policy blade: break-glass accounts and report-only mode discipline. Break-glass accounts are cloud-only Global Administrator accounts excluded from every Conditional Access policy without exception. They exist to recover access if a misconfigured policy locks out all admins. Exclude them by account object, not by group, because group membership can change.
Report-only mode is mandatory as the first state for any new policy. It runs full policy evaluation and logs results without enforcing any action. Outcomes appear under Sign-in logs > Report-only and carry one of four states: Success, Failure, User action required, and Not applied. Microsoft recommends at least one week in report-only before switching to enforcement. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access report-only mode”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Two caveats worth knowing early. The CA Insights workbook (which visualises report-only trends over time) requires Azure Monitor integration. Also, device compliance evaluation in report-only mode on macOS, iOS, and Android can prompt users to pick a certificate. Exclude those platforms from report-only device compliance rules if you don’t want unexpected end-user prompts during testing.
How Do Assignments Define Who, What, and Where a Policy Applies? Assignments are the conditional side of the if-then model. The Users assignment supports inclusions and exclusions across individual accounts, groups, directory roles, and guest or external user types. Always define both sides: who is in scope and who is explicitly excluded. Roles are particularly useful for targeting privileged access policies at Global Administrators, Security Administrators, and Exchange Administrators without building bespoke groups.
The Target resources assignment covers cloud apps, app filter groups, authentication context, and Global Secure Access. Authentication context lets you require step-up authentication for specific sensitive actions inside an app, such as accessing a high-sensitivity SharePoint site, without requiring stronger auth for the entire application.
Conditions refine assignment scope further. The key conditions are:
Device platforms: apply different controls to Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and Linux. Client apps: distinguish browser sessions from modern auth clients or legacy authentication protocols. Filter for devices: use device attribute rules (for example, device.extensionAttribute1 -eq "Privileged") to target specific hardware classes. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Assignments”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
How Do Named Locations Strengthen Your Network Conditions? Named locations give Conditional Access a geographic and network context it would otherwise lack. Two location types are available: IP-based and Country-based. A tenant supports up to 195 named locations, each holding up to 2,000 IP ranges in CIDR notation (minimum prefix /8). IP-based locations can be marked as trusted, which improves Entra ID Protection’s risk scoring accuracy. A sign-in from a trusted location is less likely to be flagged as atypical. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Network assignment”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Country-based locations use IP geolocation data by default. Microsoft Authenticator can optionally supply GPS coordinates for the same evaluation, giving higher precision for mobile sign-ins.
VPN users are the most common source of named location confusion. The policy evaluates the VPN exit IP, not the user’s physical location. An employee working in Berlin through a UK exit node will match UK-based location policies. For organisations moving to a Zero Trust network model, Global Secure Access provides source IP restoration: the policy evaluates the user’s actual device IP rather than the VPN egress point.
One UI change to note: the assignment label was renamed from “Location” to “Network” in the Entra admin centre. Existing policies configured against the old label continue to work without changes, but new policy creation uses the updated label.
Which Grant Controls Are Available on E3 Today? Every grant control below is available with Entra ID P1, included in Microsoft 365 E3. You don’t need E5 to build a strong access control posture. The six primary grant controls cover the full range from hard block to conditional access based on device health and application management state. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Grant controls”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Block access is the simplest and most absolute control. When a Block policy applies, no other grant policy overrides it.
Require MFA triggers the user’s configured authentication method. Windows Hello for Business satisfies this control for compliant Windows devices.
Require authentication strength is a more precise version of “Require MFA.” Three built-in tiers are available:
Tier Allowed Methods MFA strength Password + Authenticator push, FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business, Certificate-Based Auth (MFA), Temporary Access Pass, OATH tokens Passwordless MFA strength Authenticator phone sign-in, FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business, Certificate-Based Auth (MFA) Phishing-resistant MFA strength FIDO2 security key, Windows Hello for Business / platform credential, Certificate-Based Auth (MFA) Custom authentication strengths are available when built-in tiers don’t match your method mix. One constraint: you cannot combine “Require MFA” and “Require authentication strength” in the same policy. Pick one. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Authentication strengths”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Require device marked as compliant relies on Intune compliance policies. Supported platforms are Windows 10+, iOS, Android, macOS, and Linux Ubuntu. An Intune licence is required separately; it doesn’t come with Entra ID P1 alone.
Require Microsoft Entra hybrid joined device applies only to Windows devices joined to both on-premises Active Directory and Entra ID. iOS, Android, and macOS are excluded from this control.
Require app protection policy enforces Intune Mobile Application Management (MAM). iOS and Android are generally available. Windows is in preview. This control is the correct migration target for any policy that previously used “Require approved client app.”
Deprecation alert: “Require approved client app” was retired in March 2026. Any existing Conditional Access policy that uses this grant control as the only option must be migrated to “Require app protection policy.” If an OR combination of both controls exists in a policy, it continues to work while apps are updated. Three apps do not support app protection policy: Kaizala, Skype for Business, and Visio. For those apps, “Require approved client app” must remain where still applicable. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Grant controls”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Licence: All grant controls in this section (including authentication strengths) require Entra ID P1, included in Microsoft 365 E3, Microsoft 365 F1/F3, Microsoft 365 Business Premium, and EMS E3. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What Session Controls Can You Apply with a P1 Licence? Session controls adjust what authenticated users can do after sign-in, without blocking access entirely. Four session controls are available on Entra ID P1. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Session controls”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Sign-in frequency sets how often a user must reauthenticate. You can require reauthentication on every access attempt (useful for privileged role sessions) or set a configurable interval in hours or days. The default persistent session behaviour in most browsers keeps users signed in far longer than security posture warrants for sensitive applications.
Persistent browser session controls whether the browser stores a persistent session cookie. Setting this to “Never persistent” forces reauthentication when the browser closes, which is appropriate for shared or unmanaged devices.
App-enforced restrictions delegate session control to SharePoint Online and Exchange Online directly. SharePoint uses this to enforce limited-access views, for example blocking downloads from unmanaged devices without needing Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps.
Conditional Access App Control is covered in the next section. It appears as a session control toggle in the CA policy interface, but it requires a separate licence not included with Entra ID P1.
How Does Conditional Access App Control Work, and What Does It Cost? Conditional Access App Control uses a reverse proxy architecture to sit between the user’s browser and the target application. Non-Edge browsers access the app through a URL with the *.mcas.ms suffix appended. Microsoft Edge gets native in-browser protection without the URL modification. The proxy intercepts session activity and can apply granular session policies regardless of whether the app is managed by your organisation. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access App Control”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
The feature works with SAML 2.0 and OIDC apps, including third-party SaaS applications onboarded to Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps (MDCA). It also works with on-premises apps published through the Entra application proxy.
Session policy activities available through CAAC:
Prevent data exfiltration: block download, cut, copy, and print within the session. Protect on download: apply Microsoft Purview sensitivity labels to downloaded files instead of blocking them entirely. If you haven’t yet configured those labels, see the guide on how to configure DLP policies in Microsoft 365. Prevent upload of unlabeled files: stop users from uploading unclassified content to sanctioned apps. Block potential malware uploads: scan uploads for known malware signatures before they reach the app. Monitor sessions: log all session activity for compliance and investigation without enforcing any block. Two limitations catch organisations by surprise. First, the Teams desktop application is NOT supported for CAAC. Browser sessions only. If your session policy targets Teams, unmanaged users on the desktop client fall outside the proxy entirely. Second, session policies have a 50 MB file size limit. Files above that threshold bypass session inspection entirely. Design download controls with this limit in mind when handling large engineering or finance files.
Licence: Conditional Access App Control requires Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps. This is included in Microsoft 365 E5 Security, Microsoft 365 E5, and EMS E5. It is NOT included in Microsoft 365 E3 or standalone Entra ID P1/P2 licences. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access App Control”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What Does Upgrading to E5 Actually Unlock? Risk-Based Conditional Access Risk-based Conditional Access is the primary identity security upgrade that E5 unlocks. Entra ID Protection runs a continuous machine-learning analysis of every sign-in and user account in your tenant. It surfaces two risk signals: sign-in risk (the probability that a specific authentication attempt is not from the legitimate user) and user risk (the probability that a user account has been compromised). Both require Entra ID P2. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Risk-based Conditional Access policies”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Sign-In Risk Policy Microsoft recommends targeting medium AND high sign-in risk levels. Low risk generates too much noise for a production enforcement policy. The recommended grant configuration pairs authentication strength (built-in MFA strength) with a session control of sign-in frequency: every time, forcing full reauthentication on each risky sign-in.
For passwordless deployments, use Passwordless MFA strength or Phishing-resistant MFA strength instead, so users who have already removed passwords aren’t prompted to use a method they don’t have.
One critical prerequisite: users who have not completed MFA registration and trigger a risky sign-in receive error AADSTS53004 and are completely blocked. They cannot self-remediate. Ensure organisation-wide MFA registration is complete before enabling sign-in risk enforcement. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access policy: Sign-in risk”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
User Risk Policy Microsoft recommends targeting high user risk only. Medium user risk generates too many false positives for automated enforcement. The grant for user risk is “Require risk remediation”, which automatically applies authentication strength and sets sign-in frequency to every time.
User risk enables Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) self-remediation. The user resets their password, Entra ID Protection reassesses the risk level, and the account is unblocked without helpdesk involvement. This is the operational efficiency argument for P2 beyond the security posture improvement. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access policy: User risk”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Exclude service accounts and break-glass accounts from both risk policies. Service accounts typically exhibit atypical sign-in patterns by design, and risk policies will generate constant alerts for them.
The real upgrade value is continuous, automated response. Without P2, your process for responding to a compromised credential is: detection, alert, analyst review, ticket opened, helpdesk calls the user. With P2, the policy triggers MFA remediation or SSPR the moment the risk fires. The attack window shrinks from hours to seconds.
Licence: Sign-in risk and user risk conditions in Conditional Access require Entra ID P2, included in Microsoft 365 E5, Microsoft Defender Suite, and EMS E5. Entra ID P2 is also available as an add-on for Microsoft 365 E3. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
How Do You Roll Out Conditional Access Policies Safely? A phased rollout is not optional. CA policies are additive, and any applicable Block policy wins regardless of the order policies are evaluated. Rushing to enforcement before validating report-only results is the most common cause of lockout incidents. The three-phase model below applies to any new policy, not just a full deployment. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Plan a Conditional Access deployment”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Phase Timeline Policies Minimum Report-Only Period Phase 1 Weeks 1-2 Block legacy authentication, secure MFA registration, phishing-resistant MFA for privileged roles 1 week Phase 2 Weeks 2-3 Strong authentication for all users and guests, app protection policy for mobile, require MFA for device join/registration 1 week Phase 3 Weeks 3-4 Sign-in risk policy, user risk policy, token protection, device code flow restriction (P2 required) 1 week Phase 1 is the highest-value starting point. Legacy authentication protocols (Basic Auth, IMAP, POP3, SMTP Auth) bypass modern CA entirely. Securing the MFA registration page closes the registration loophole: without it, an attacker with a stolen password can register their own MFA device before the legitimate user notices. Use FIDO2 or Windows Hello for Business for privileged roles, not Authenticator push.
Phase 2 extends strong authentication to the broader user population. App protection policy for iOS and Android closes the unmanaged mobile access gap without requiring device enrolment in Intune.
Phase 3 requires Entra ID P2. Token protection and device code flow restriction reduce token theft and phishing-resistant bypass techniques. Every phase starts in report-only. No exceptions.
Licence Comparison: E3, E5, and the P2 Add-On The table below maps each Conditional Access feature to its minimum licence requirement. Use it as a scoping reference before your rollout kickoff call. Features that appear gated may simply require an add-on you already have but haven’t assigned. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Feature E3 (Entra P1) Entra P2 add-on on E3 E5 (Entra P2) MDCA (separate) Standard CA policies Yes Yes Yes N/A Named locations Yes Yes Yes N/A Authentication strengths Yes Yes Yes N/A Report-only mode Yes Yes Yes N/A Device compliance grant (Intune) Yes* Yes* Yes* N/A App protection policy grant Yes Yes Yes N/A Sign-in risk condition No Yes Yes N/A User risk condition No Yes Yes N/A Entra ID Protection reports No Yes Yes N/A CA App Control (MCAS) No No No** Yes *Intune licence required separately for device compliance.
**Included in M365 E5 Security / E5 which bundles MDCA; standalone E5 without the Security add-on may require MDCA separately. Verify your current bundle against the Microsoft product terms.
The Entra P2 add-on for E3 is the most cost-efficient path to risk-based CA without a full E5 migration. It delivers sign-in risk, user risk, and Entra ID Protection reporting for identity security teams that don’t yet need the full E5 workload bundle.
Frequently Asked Questions What is the difference between report-only mode and disabled in Conditional Access? A disabled policy is completely inactive and logs nothing. Report-only mode runs full policy evaluation and logs the outcome for every matching sign-in, but does not enforce any grant or session control. The four logged outcome states are: Success, Failure, User action required, and Not applied. Microsoft recommends at least one week in report-only before enforcement for any new policy. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Report-only mode”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Can Microsoft 365 E3 users configure risk-based Conditional Access? No. Sign-in risk and user risk conditions require Entra ID P2. E3 includes P1 only. E3 organisations can purchase the Entra P2 add-on per user to unlock risk-based CA without upgrading to E5. Alternatively, a full upgrade to Microsoft 365 E5 includes P2 across the board. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Microsoft Entra Licensing”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
What should I do with existing “Require approved client app” policies? Migrate them to “Require app protection policy” before the retirement breaks them. The safest path: add “Require app protection policy” as an OR option alongside the existing grant, test in report-only, then remove the deprecated grant once validation is complete. Three exceptions remain: Kaizala, Skype for Business, and Visio must continue using “Require approved client app” where still applicable.
Why are users getting AADSTS53004 errors after enabling sign-in risk policies? AADSTS53004 means the user has not completed MFA registration but triggered a risky sign-in. Risk policies require MFA remediation, which is impossible without a registered MFA method. Complete MFA registration for all users before enabling sign-in risk enforcement. Run the Authentication Methods Activity report in the Entra admin centre to identify unregistered users first. Source: Microsoft Learn, “Conditional Access: Sign-in risk”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Does Conditional Access App Control work with the Microsoft Teams desktop app? No. The Teams desktop application is not supported for CA App Control. CAAC applies only to browser sessions. Users on the Teams desktop client bypass the proxy entirely. Users accessing Teams through a browser on an unmanaged device are subject to CAAC session policies. Design your unmanaged device policies with this boundary in mind. Source: Microsoft Learn, “CA App Control”, retrieved 2026-05-22.
Start in Report-Only Mode, Then Build From There Conditional Access is not a deploy-once configuration. It grows with your organisation’s licence posture, device management maturity, and risk tolerance. Start by reviewing any existing “Require approved client app” policies, since those need migration now. Audit your break-glass accounts and verify they’re excluded from every policy. Then run your first new policy in report-only for at least a week before enforcing.
For E5 evaluation: activate an Entra ID P2 trial and enable the sign-in risk policy in report-only mode for two weeks. The Entra ID Protection risky sign-ins report will show you real risk events from your tenant. That live data is far more persuasive for an upgrade business case than any vendor comparison sheet.
The Zero Trust identity layer starts with P1 and the policies you can build today. P2 adds the automated risk response layer on top. Both are worth building deliberately rather than fast. For the broader data-protection picture, see the guide on how to configure DLP policies in Microsoft 365 — Conditional Access and DLP work as complementary layers in a defence-in-depth strategy. Browse all posts in the Security and Microsoft 365 categories for related coverage on identity and access management.
summary: Every Microsoft 365 E3 organisation already holds the licence to configure powerful Conditional Access (CA) policies today. Entra ID P1 is included in M365 E3, and it covers named locations, authentication strengths, device compliance grants, and app protection controls. That’s a real Zero Trust foundation, not just an MFA toggle. What E5 adds is automated, continuous risk response: Entra ID Protection feeds real-time sign-in and user risk signals directly into CA policy decisions. This guide walks through the full feature set, from policy anatomy to safe rollout, so you can build the right policies for the licences you hold now and make a clear-eyed decision about upgrading.
title: Complete Guide: Conditional Access Policies in Entra ID

content:
Data breaches cost organizations a global average of $4.44 million in 2025, according to the IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report. For U.S.-based companies that number jumps to $10.22 million. Organizations that deployed AI and automation in their security workflows saved an average of $1.9 million per breach in the same report. Microsoft 365 Data Loss Prevention (DLP) is one of the most direct controls you can place between your sensitive data and an incident. This guide walks through every step: portal navigation, policy anatomy, scoping, sensitive information types, simulation mode, tuning, and monitoring.
Properly configured DLP policies are your organization’s first technical control against accidental and intentional data exposure.
Key Takeaways
Data breaches cost a global average of $4.44M in 2025 (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2025). DLP policies are configured exclusively in the Microsoft Purview portal (purview.microsoft.com). Always start in simulation mode before enforcing; phased rollouts in 3 steps cut user disruption. Teams, Endpoint, and Copilot DLP all require E5 or the E5 Compliance add-on. Shadow AI added $670,000 to average breach costs in 2025, making Copilot-scoped DLP urgent. What Is DLP in Microsoft 365 and Why Does It Matter in 2025? DLP policies detect and block the accidental or intentional sharing of sensitive data across email, SharePoint, Teams, endpoints, and Copilot. Configure them correctly and you have a technical control that covers both external breach vectors and the negligent insider — who caused 55% of 2025 insider incidents (Ponemon Institute / DTEX 2025 Cost of Insider Risks Report, 2025).
Insider threats are a separate, compounding risk. The average annualized cost of an insider incident hit $17.4 million in 2025, with 55% of incidents caused by negligent employees rather than malicious actors (Ponemon Institute / DTEX 2025 Cost of Insider Risks Report, 2025). Your DLP policy is often the only technical control that catches a careless employee attaching a client spreadsheet to a personal email.
The scale of the external threat is just as stark. Verizon’s 2025 DBIR recorded 12,195 confirmed data breaches, the highest single-year count ever, with ransomware appearing in 44% of all breaches and third-party involvement doubling to 30% (Verizon 2025 Data Breach Investigations Report, 2025). DLP covers the internal and outbound vectors that perimeter controls miss.
The shadow AI problem sharpens the urgency further. IBM’s 2025 report found that shadow AI usage added $670,000 to average breach costs, and 1 in 5 breaches involved shadow AI. With Microsoft 365 Copilot now generally available, you need DLP scoped to Copilot or you have a blind spot.
Average Annualized Cost of Insider Threat Incidents (2025) Source: Ponemon Institute / DTEX 2025 Cost of Insider Risks Report Average Annualized Cost of Insider Threat Incidents (2025) Negligent employee Credential theft Malicious insider $8.8M $4.8M $3.7M Source: Ponemon Institute / DTEX 2025 Cost of Insider Risks Report Where Does DLP Live: Navigating the Microsoft Purview Compliance Portal? DLP policies are configured at purview.microsoft.com. Once inside, the path is: Data loss prevention in the left navigation, then Policies, then Create policy. The old Microsoft 365 Compliance Center (compliance.microsoft.com) now redirects to Purview; it is no longer the active admin surface.
If you cannot see the DLP menu, check your role assignment. You need the DLP Compliance Management or Compliance Administrator role in Microsoft Entra ID. The Security Reader role gives read-only access to existing policies but blocks creation.
DLP Policy Anatomy: Rules, Conditions, Actions, and Notifications A DLP policy has three tiers: the Policy sets scope (which workloads and users are targeted), the Rule contains matching logic (a single policy can have multiple rules at different severity thresholds), and Conditions plus Actions are the detection and response mechanisms inside each rule. Understanding this hierarchy before clicking “Create policy” prevents structural mistakes that require rebuilding from scratch.
The Policy layer defines scope: which workloads (Exchange, SharePoint, Teams, etc.) and which users or groups the policy targets. The Rule layer contains the logic. A single policy can hold multiple rules with different severity thresholds, so you can audit low-count matches and block high-count matches from the same policy object.
Conditions specify what DLP looks for: sensitive information types (SITs), sensitivity labels, keywords, sender domain, recipient domain, or a combination. Actions specify what happens when conditions match.
DLP Action Types and Enforcement Level Action Enforcement Level User Impact Audit only Logging only None visible Block with override Soft block Warning; user can proceed with justification Block Hard block Action is stopped; no override available User notifications and policy tips are the in-app messages end users see in Outlook, Teams, or SharePoint when a rule matches. A well-written policy tip explains what triggered the warning and what the user should do, which reduces help desk tickets significantly.
Should You Use Built-In Templates or Build Custom DLP Policies? Built-in templates are the fastest starting point, but they nearly always need tuning before you enforce them. Microsoft ships templates for more than a dozen regulatory and industry frameworks, grouped into categories including Financial data (PCI DSS, credit card numbers), Medical and health (HIPAA), and Privacy (GDPR, U.S. state privacy laws, and others).
Use a custom policy when your organization has proprietary data types, internal project codes, or when a built-in template generates too many false positives out of the box. Custom policies let you define your own SITs with regex patterns and keyword lists.
One common trap: default match thresholds for some SITs are set to “any 1 instance.” A credit card SIT configured with a threshold of 1 will fire on a test email with a single dummy number. Always review and raise the confidence level and instance count before moving out of simulation mode.
How Do You Choose the Right Scope: Exchange, SharePoint, Teams, Endpoints, and Copilot? Where you apply the policy determines which user activities DLP can detect. Selecting the right locations is the single most important scoping decision you will make. Below is the full location list with license requirements.
Location What DLP Can Detect Minimum License Exchange Email Sent/received messages, attachments M365 E3 SharePoint Files at rest in SharePoint libraries M365 E3 OneDrive Files at rest in personal OneDrive accounts M365 E3 Microsoft Teams Chat and channel messages M365 E5 or E5 Compliance add-on Endpoint devices File copy to USB, print, clipboard, network share M365 E5 or E5 Compliance add-on Microsoft 365 Copilot Blocks Copilot from grounding on labeled/sensitive files M365 E5 or E5 Compliance add-on Microsoft 365 Copilot DLP reached general availability in June 2025. It prevents Copilot from using labeled or sensitive files as grounding data when responding to prompts, which closes a gap that shadow AI risk analysis identified as costing organizations an average of $670,000 per breach (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report, 2025).
Sensitive Information Types: Built-In, Custom, and Exact Data Match A sensitive information type (SIT) is the detection engine inside a DLP condition: it defines the pattern to match, supporting keywords, and a confidence level — and the confidence level you choose determines whether your policy catches real threats or generates alert floods on day one. Microsoft ships over 350 built-in SITs covering credit card numbers, Social Security numbers, passport numbers, and medical record numbers.
Custom SITs let you define proprietary patterns. If your organization uses a project code format like PRJ-XXXX-YYYY, you can build a custom SIT with that regex and tie a DLP rule to it.
Exact Data Match (EDM) goes further. It fingerprints a structured dataset, such as an employee ID database or customer list, so DLP can detect exact values from that dataset with near-zero false positives. EDM requires E5 Compliance.
Confidence levels matter in practice. High confidence matches are more specific and generate fewer false positives. Medium and Low confidence matches cast a wider net and are better suited for audit-only rules where you want visibility without user disruption.
How Do You Deploy DLP Safely Using Simulation Mode? Simulation mode lets you run full policy evaluation across all locations before any enforcement action fires.
Always start in simulation mode. Organizations that skip it and go straight to enforcement typically spend the first week managing override requests rather than doing security work. Simulation runs full policy evaluation across all configured locations without enforcing any action, generating matched-item data you can inspect before users are affected. Simulation mode replaced the old “Test” mode as of the November 2025 Microsoft Purview update (Microsoft Learn, retrieved 2026-05-22).
The simulation dashboard has three tabs: Matched items (files and messages that triggered the policy), Alerts (rule-level alerts generated during simulation), and Scan status (which locations have been evaluated). Simulations run for up to 15 days; results are retained for 30 days.
The recommended phased rollout has three steps:
DLP Phased Deployment Approach Phase State Scope User Impact 1 Simulation All locations None 2 Simulation + Policy Tips Pilot group (50 users) Notifications only 3 Full Enforcement All target users Blocked or audited Use PowerShell to check the current mode and priority of all policies in your tenant:
# Connect to Security & Compliance PowerShell (ExchangeOnlineManagement v3) Connect-IPPSSession # List all DLP policies with their current mode and priority Get-DlpCompliancePolicy | Select-Object Name, Mode, Priority Mode will return TestWithNotifications (Phase 2), TestWithoutNotifications (Phase 1), or Enable (Phase 3). Confirm the correct phase before promoting to enforcement.
How Do You Tune DLP Policies to Reduce False Positives? False positives are the primary reason DLP policies get disabled — and the most common trigger is a broad SIT with an instance count of 1. One alert flood during a product launch and the policy is off by end of day. The tuning workflow below works from simulation data rather than gut feel, which is the only reliable way to predict match volume before enforcement begins.
Open Activity Explorer in the Purview portal and filter by your policy name during the simulation window. Identify which SIT is generating the most unexpected matches. Raise the confidence level from Medium to High for that SIT in the rule condition. Raise the instance count. For broad SITs like Credit Card Number, require 5 or more instances rather than 1. Add exceptions: exclude specific sender domains, SharePoint site URLs, or security groups that generate legitimate matches. Re-run simulation after each change and review the new matched-item count before enforcing. The Block with Override action is useful during early enforcement. Users who receive a block can enter a business justification and proceed anyway. You capture the justification in Activity Explorer and can identify legitimate workflows that need exceptions before tightening the policy further.
How Do You Monitor DLP Activity with Activity Explorer and Defender? Activity Explorer and DLP Alerts work together to give you a full picture of policy matches across all Microsoft 365 locations. Credit: Tima Miroshnichenko via Pexels.
Monitoring is where DLP earns its place in your security stack. Activity Explorer and DLP Alerts are the two primary tools, and they serve different purposes. Verizon’s 2025 DBIR analyzed 12,195 confirmed data breaches, the highest single-year count on record (Verizon 2025 Data Breach Investigations Report, 2025). Alert fatigue from a misconfigured DLP policy makes your detection posture worse, not better.
Activity Explorer (purview.microsoft.com, then Data classification, then Activity explorer) shows every DLP activity by user, location, SIT, policy, and action. Filter by date range and activity type to build a weekly or monthly review cadence.
DLP Alerts are configured at the rule level. When a rule match occurs in enforcement mode, an alert routes to the DLP Alerts dashboard in the Purview portal. Set alert thresholds carefully: one alert per event for high-severity rules, aggregated alerts for lower-severity ones.
Microsoft Defender XDR surfaces DLP alerts as incidents at security.microsoft.com. This is useful for correlating a DLP match (a user emailed a file containing SSNs) with other Defender signals (that same user logged in from an unusual location 10 minutes earlier). Cross-correlating these signals is where the E5 security stack pays off.
The investment in monitoring infrastructure is well-supported by the data. Organizations now allocate 16.5% of their IT security budget to insider risk management, up from 8.2% in 2023. More tellingly, 65% say their insider risk program pre-empted a data breach before it could cause damage (Ponemon Institute / DTEX 2025 Cost of Insider Risks Report, 2025). A DLP monitoring cadence is a core part of that pre-emption posture.
License Requirements: What You Get at E3, E5, and the Compliance Add-On M365 E3 covers DLP for Exchange, SharePoint, and OneDrive — everything else requires E5 or the E5 Compliance add-on. If your rollout scope includes Teams, Endpoint, or Copilot DLP, verify licensing before the kickoff call. A missing E5 entitlement blocks the location from appearing in the scope selector, not the policy creation wizard, which means you can configure a policy that silently covers nothing.
Feature M365 E3 M365 E5 E5 Compliance Add-On Exchange, SharePoint, OneDrive DLP Yes Yes Yes Microsoft Teams DLP No Yes Yes Endpoint DLP No Yes Yes Trainable classifiers in DLP No Yes Yes DLP for Microsoft 365 Copilot No Yes Yes Third-party cloud app DLP No Requires Defender for Cloud Apps Requires Defender for Cloud Apps Source: Microsoft Learn (learn.microsoft.com/en-us/purview/dlp-overview-plan-for-dlp, retrieved 2026-05-22)
Organizations with mixed license pools (some users on E3, some on E5) can scope E5-only locations to the licensed group using user-scoped policies, so you don’t have to choose between full coverage and compliance.
What Are the Most Common DLP Configuration Mistakes? Most DLP failures come from five predictable mistakes. Recognizing them before rollout is faster than recovering from a failed deployment.
Mistake 1: Starting in enforcement mode, not simulation. The first policy a new admin creates should always go into simulation. Jumping straight to enforcement triggers immediate user complaints and a wave of override requests that overwhelm the help desk.
Mistake 2: Leaving instance count at 1 for broad SITs. The Credit Card Number SIT will match any 16-digit number. Set the instance count to 5 or higher for SITs that cover common number patterns.
Mistake 3: Forgetting that Teams requires E5. IT admins regularly discover Teams chat is uncovered after rolling out Exchange and SharePoint DLP. Confirm licensing before the project kickoff, not after.
Mistake 4: No exclusions for internal tools. Automated reports, DevOps pipelines, and HR systems often generate files that match SIT patterns. Broad policies catch these and generate noise. Scope exclusions during Phase 1 simulation.
Mistake 5: No monitoring cadence. A policy set and forgotten drifts. New workflows emerge. SIT patterns evolve. Schedule a monthly Activity Explorer review as a recurring calendar item when you go live.
Frequently Asked Questions What is the difference between a DLP policy and a DLP rule? A DLP policy is the container: it defines which workloads and users are in scope. A DLP rule is the logic inside the policy: it specifies conditions (what to detect) and actions (what to do when a match occurs). One policy can contain multiple rules with different thresholds, so a single policy can both audit low-count and block high-count matches.
Does Microsoft 365 E3 include DLP? Yes. M365 E3 includes DLP for Exchange Online, SharePoint Online, and OneDrive for Business. It does not include DLP for Microsoft Teams, Endpoint DLP, or DLP for Microsoft 365 Copilot. Those locations require M365 E5 or the E5 Compliance add-on. Check the Microsoft licensing documentation for your current agreement before scoping a rollout.
Can DLP policies block files in Microsoft Teams? Yes, but only with the right license. Teams DLP requires M365 E5 or the E5 Compliance add-on. Once licensed, DLP can detect sensitive content in Teams chat and channel messages, including attachments. Without the correct license, Teams is simply not available as a location in the policy scope selector.
What is simulation mode in Microsoft Purview DLP? Simulation mode runs full DLP policy evaluation across all selected locations without enforcing any action. It replaced the legacy “Test” mode as of the November 2025 Purview update. Simulations run for up to 15 days, results are retained for 30 days, and a dedicated dashboard shows matched items, alerts, and scan status. Always use simulation before enabling enforcement.
How long does it take for a new DLP policy to take effect? A new DLP policy typically propagates across Exchange Online within 1 hour. SharePoint and OneDrive policies can take up to 24 hours to fully propagate, particularly for content already at rest in large libraries. Endpoint DLP propagation depends on device check-in intervals. Use the Purview portal’s scan status tab in simulation mode to confirm propagation before enforcing (Microsoft Learn, dlp-create-deploy-policy, retrieved 2026-05-22).
Simulate First, Enforce When Ready Configuring DLP in Microsoft 365 is not a one-time task. It’s an ongoing practice: build a simulation baseline, tune against real matched data, enforce in phases, and review monthly. The cost of getting it wrong is measurable: $4.44 million average per breach globally, $17.4 million average for insider incidents, and $670,000 in additional exposure if shadow AI is left uncontrolled (IBM, Ponemon / DTEX, 2025).
Start with Exchange and SharePoint (E3 is enough), validate your SITs in simulation, and expand to Teams, Endpoint, and Copilot as your E5 licensing allows. The phased deployment model in this guide gives you the control to do that without disrupting users or triggering a policy rollback.
If you’re building DLP as part of a broader Microsoft Purview compliance program, the next logical step is configuring sensitivity labels and auto-labeling policies, which feed directly into DLP conditions and strengthen detection accuracy without increasing the false-positive rate. Browse all posts in the Security and Microsoft 365 categories, or filter by Microsoft Purview and Compliance for related coverage.
summary: Data breaches cost organizations a global average of $4.44 million in 2025, according to the IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report. For U.S.-based companies that number jumps to $10.22 million. Organizations that deployed AI and automation in their security workflows saved an average of $1.9 million per breach in the same report. Microsoft 365 Data Loss Prevention (DLP) is one of the most direct controls you can place between your sensitive data and an incident. This guide walks through every step: portal navigation, policy anatomy, scoping, sensitive information types, simulation mode, tuning, and monitoring.
title: How to Configure DLP Policies in Microsoft 365
